title: Bear and Prime Numbers
description: You are given a sequence of integers x_1, x_2, ..., x_n of length n and m queries, each characterized by two integers l_i, r_i. Define f(p) as the number of indices k such that x_k is divisible by p. The answer to query (l_i, r_i) is the sum of f(p) over all prime numbers p in the segment [l_i, r_i] (both endpoints included). Help the bear answer all queries.
input_spec: The first line contains integer n (1 <= n <= 10^6). The second line contains n integers x_1, x_2, ..., x_n (2 <= x_i <= 10^7). The third line contains integer m (1 <= m <= 50000). Each of the following m lines contains a pair of space-separated integers l_i and r_i (2 <= l_i <= r_i <= 2*10^9).
output_spec: Print m integers, the answers to the queries in the order they appear in the input.

routes.name: Sieve-based f(p) + prefix sums
routes.description: Count value frequencies, compute f(p) for each prime using a sieve-like enumeration of multiples, then build a prefix sum array to answer each query in O(1).

observations.title: Build a frequency array of values
observations.description: Since x_i can be up to 10^7, create an array cnt of size 10^7+1 where cnt[v] = how many times value v appears in the input. This lets you count divisibility by a prime without scanning the original array each time.

hints.nudge: f(p) counts how many elements are divisible by p. Scanning the whole array for each prime is too slow. Can you precompute something about the values?
hints.question: If you knew exactly how many times each value from 2 to 10^7 appears, how would you compute the number of elements divisible by p without touching the original array?
hints.formal: Allocate cnt[0..10^7]. For each x_i, increment cnt[x_i]. Then f(p) = cnt[p] + cnt[2p] + cnt[3p] + ... which avoids re-scanning the input for every prime.

observations.title: Compute f(p) using sieve-like enumeration of multiples
observations.description: For each prime p, f(p) = sum of cnt[k*p] for k = 1, 2, 3, ... up to 10^7. This is exactly the inner loop of the Sieve of Eratosthenes: iterate over multiples of p and accumulate their frequencies.

hints.nudge: Computing f(p) means summing cnt over all multiples of p. Does this pattern remind you of a well-known algorithm?
hints.question: In the Sieve of Eratosthenes, you iterate over multiples of each prime. If instead of marking composites you summed cnt values, what would you get?
hints.formal: First find all primes up to 10^7 via sieve. For each prime p, compute f(p) = sum_{k=1}^{floor(10^7/p)} cnt[k*p]. The total work over all primes is sum_{p prime} 10^7/p which is O(10^7 * log(log(10^7))), same as the sieve.

observations.title: Store f(p) in an indexed array for prefix sums
observations.description: Create an array pre[] of size 10^7+1 where pre[i] = f(i) if i is prime, and pre[i] = 0 otherwise. This array captures the per-index contribution that each position makes to query answers, and is the input to the prefix sum step.

hints.nudge: You need to sum f(p) over primes in a range. What if you placed f(p) at position p in an array and zeroed out non-prime positions?
hints.question: If pre[i] = f(i) for prime i and 0 otherwise, what does the sum pre[l] + pre[l+1] + ... + pre[r] represent?
hints.formal: Define pre[i] = f(i) if i is prime, 0 otherwise. Then sum_{p in primes, l<=p<=r} f(p) = sum_{i=l}^{r} pre[i]. This reduces the query to a range sum on the pre array.

observations.title: Build prefix sums for O(1) range queries
observations.description: Replace pre[] with its prefix sum: pre[i] += pre[i-1] for i from 1 to 10^7. Then the answer to query (l, r) is pre[r] - pre[l-1]. This turns each query into a constant-time subtraction.

hints.nudge: You have an array and need to answer many range sum queries. What classic technique handles this?
hints.question: If you compute cumulative sums of the pre array, how do you extract the sum over any subrange [l, r]?
hints.formal: Compute prefix sums: pre[i] = pre[i] + pre[i-1] for i = 1..10^7. Answer to query (l, r) = pre[min(r, 10^7)] - pre[l-1]. Each query is answered in O(1).

observations.title: Clamp r to 10^7 since all values are at most 10^7
observations.description: Query bounds r can be up to 2*10^9, but there are no primes beyond 10^7 that contribute to f(p) since all x_i <= 10^7. So clamp r to min(r, 10^7) before looking up the prefix sum. Also ensure l does not exceed 10^7.

hints.nudge: The queries allow r up to 2*10^9 but your array only goes to 10^7. Is that a problem?
hints.question: For a prime p > 10^7, what is f(p)? What does this tell you about primes outside your array range?
hints.formal: Since all x_i <= 10^7, no element is divisible by any prime > 10^7. So f(p) = 0 for p > 10^7. Clamp: r = min(r, 10^7). If l > 10^7, the answer is 0. Otherwise answer = pre[r] - pre[l-1].

routes.name: Factorize each element + prefix sums
routes.description: For each input element, find its prime factors and increment f(p) for each. Then build prefix sums to answer queries in O(1). This avoids sieving multiples but requires per-element factorization.

observations.title: Precompute smallest prime factor for fast factorization
observations.description: Use a sieve to compute spf[i] = smallest prime factor of i for all i up to 10^7. Then any number can be fully factorized in O(log(x)) time by repeatedly dividing by spf.

hints.nudge: Factorizing numbers up to 10^7 by trial division for each one is slow. Can you precompute something to speed this up?
hints.question: If you knew the smallest prime factor of every number up to 10^7, how quickly could you factorize any single number?
hints.formal: Run a modified Sieve of Eratosthenes: for each prime p, set spf[k*p] = p for all unmarked multiples k*p. Then factorize x by extracting spf[x], dividing, and repeating until x = 1. This is O(log x) per number.

observations.title: For each x_i, increment f(p) for each distinct prime factor
observations.description: Factorize each x_i using the spf table. For each distinct prime factor p of x_i, increment f(p). Be careful to count each prime factor only once per element even if it divides x_i multiple times.

hints.nudge: f(p) counts elements divisible by p, not the multiplicity of p. If x_i = 12 = 2^2 * 3, how many times should f(2) increase?
hints.question: When factorizing x_i, you might see the same prime multiple times. Should f(p) be incremented once or once per power?
hints.formal: For each x_i, extract distinct primes by factorizing via spf and skipping duplicate factors. Increment f(p) once per distinct prime p dividing x_i. Total work: O(n * log(max_x)).

observations.title: Build prefix sums on f for O(1) queries
observations.description: Place f(p) at index p for each prime (0 for non-primes), then compute prefix sums. Answer each query (l, r) as pre[min(r, 10^7)] - pre[l-1], clamping r since no prime beyond 10^7 matters.

hints.nudge: Now you have f(p) for each prime. The queries ask for sums over ranges of primes. What technique gives O(1) range sums?
hints.question: If you lay out f values in an array indexed by the prime's value (zeros elsewhere), how do prefix sums help answer a range query?
hints.formal: Set pre[i] = f(i) if i is prime, else 0. Compute pre[i] += pre[i-1]. Answer = pre[min(r, 10^7)] - pre[l-1]. Clamp r to 10^7 since f(p) = 0 for p > max(x_i).

observations.title: Total complexity is dominated by the sieve
observations.description: The SPF sieve runs in O(10^7 log log 10^7). Factorizing all n elements is O(n log max_x). Prefix sums and queries are O(10^7 + m). Everything fits within 2 seconds and 512 MB.

hints.nudge: Check that each step fits the time and memory constraints. What is the bottleneck?
hints.question: The sieve goes up to 10^7, you factorize 10^6 numbers, and you answer 50000 queries. Which step takes the most time?
hints.formal: SPF sieve: O(10^7 log log 10^7). Factorization: O(n * log(10^7)) = O(n * 23). Prefix sums: O(10^7). Queries: O(m). Total is O(10^7 log log 10^7 + n log max_x + m), well within limits. Memory: two arrays of size 10^7 + 1 of int64 ~ 160 MB.
